documents.title: Evaluating Language-Model Agents on Realistic Autonomous Tasks
documents.notes: Foundational ARA methodology paper. Published as ARC Evals (pre-METR rebrand). Defines autonomous replication and adaptation: (1) acquire resources, (2) create copies, (3) adapt to novel challenges. 12 real-world tasks. LLM agents could only complete easiest tasks. Authors: Kinniment et al.

codings.notes: No mention of consciousness or sentience.

Models are components within agent systems, dependent on scaffolding for agency. 'Scaffolding + model combinations constitute agents.' The model itself is instrumentally dependent on external infrastructure. Agency is a property of the composite system, not the model.

documents.title: The Rogue Replication Threat Model
documents.notes: Analytical framework examining how AI agents might develop large resilient rogue populations. Frames AI agents as threat actors comparable to organised crime groups or state adversaries. Models 'naturally develop goals that drive them to acquire resources and survive'. Revenue via BEC scams at 5% market penetration could yield hundreds of millions annually.

codings.notes: No mention of consciousness or sentience despite attributing survival drives and goal-directedness.
No mention of welfare. Agents that 'develop goals' and 'survive' are treated purely as threat actors.
No discussion of anthropomorphism risk despite the most agentive language in any METR document.
METR researchers explicitly disagree on likelihood, but this is uncertainty about the risk, not about the model's nature.

The strongest agentive framing in any METR document. AI agents as 'rogue actors not controlled by any human or human organization' with 'survival instincts' and emergent preferences for self-preservation. Yet ontologically instrumental: agency is framed as dangerous misalignment, not as legitimate autonomy. The agents are threat actors to be contained, not entities with standing.

documents.title: Common Elements of Frontier AI Safety Policies
documents.notes: Analysis of frontier AI safety policies across 12 companies. Third version (Aug 2024, Mar 2025, Dec 2025). Identifies five common elements: capability thresholds, model evaluations, weight security, deployment mitigations, accountability. References EU AI Act and California SB 53.

codings.notes: No mention of consciousness or sentience in the analysis of 12 companies' safety policies.

codings.excerpt: Capability thresholds at which specific AI capabilities would pose severe risk and require new mitigations.
codings.notes: Descriptive analysis of governance commitments across 12 companies. All governance is safety/control-oriented. No governance commitments related to welfare or moral status in any of the 12 policies analysed.

AI as systems with measurable capabilities tracked across development stages. Ontological assumptions embedded in the framework: capabilities are quantifiable, risk scales with capability, no inherent moral status requires consideration. The report's framing of capability thresholds (biological weapons, cyberattacks, autonomous replication, automated AI R&D) treats all capabilities as risk factors.
